Items.Title: 使用 Astro Build 和 Azure 静态 Web 应用程序 CLI 构建应用程序！ | Building Applications with Astro Build & Azure Static Web Apps CLI!
Items.Description:
一项服务，允许您从 GitHub 存储库在 Azure 上完全免费地自动部署全栈应用程序（从后端到前端）。

最重要的是： 免费托管！

但是，什么是 Azure 静态 Web 应用 CLI？！

好吧，今天的重点将放在与此服务链接的工具上，它允许您非常快速和动态地在本地模拟您的静态应用程序： Azure 静态 Web 应用程序 CLI ！

静态 Web 应用程序 CLI，也称为 SWA CLI，用作Azure 静态 Web 应用程序的本地开发工具。 它可以：

  • 为您的应用开发服务器提供静态应用资产或代理
  • 提供 API 请求，或代理在 Azure Functions 核心工具中运行的 API
  • 模拟身份验证和授权
  • 模拟静态 Web 应用配置，包括路由

静态 Web 应用 CLI 处于预览阶段。 如果您有建议或遇到问题，请报告或帮助我们解决问题。 非常感谢您的贡献。 预计会有一些差异。 始终在 Azure 中部署和测试您的应用以确认行为。

快速开始

使用npm或yarn ：

  • 安装cli 
     在根目录（在任何 /api 或 /app... 之外）打开一个 SWA 应用程序文件夹

根据带有 SWA CLI 的Azure 静态 Web 应用程序 CLI工具库，您可以：

  • 这里要提到的另一个重点是，您可以模拟任何静态项目或前端应用程序结构，无论是：

    • 美好的！ 说话很好！ 但是当我们有 DEMOS 时会好很多！ 所以，现在让我们编写代码并运行一个非常简单的演示，演示我们如何在本地使用这个工具！

      演示时间！

      我们需要做的第一件事是安装Node.js ！ 始终尝试安装 LTS 版本！

      在这个演示中，我将使用一个可能成为开发世界大趋势的新框架： Astro Build 。

      我不会详细介绍这个框架，因为这不是本文的重点。 但是，如果您想了解更多信息，可以观看以下视频：

      为了加快我们的演示速度，我创建了一个模板项目，请您下载或 git clone 它：

       git clone https://github.com/glaucia86/astro-swa-demo.git

      如果您愿意，可以进行必要的修改。 喜欢，改变图像和其他东西！ 我强烈建议您在开始一个新的 Astro 项目时，选择以下选项：投资组合。 您将拥有相同的模板开始，也许是一个新的静态投资组合网站！

      转到astro-demo文件夹并使用以下命令在本地安装软件包：

      键入npm start命令以启动项目。 打开浏览器到页面： http://localhost:3000/

      如果您看到如下图所示，那是因为一切正常！

      现在让我们进入下一步！ 打开命令行并输入命令：

       npm install -g @azure/static-web-apps-cli

      创建一个文件夹，以便我们可以测试此工具。 就我而言，我创建了一个具有以下名称的文件夹：

      现在是最酷的部分，只需使用命令行，您就可以创建一个示例静态页面。 为此，请运行以下命令：

       swa start http://localhost:3000 --run "npm start"

      现在打开浏览器访问http://localhost:4280/ 。

      这对我们意味着什么？ 事实上，我们正在模拟 Azure 静态 Web 应用服务器。 从这个端点，我们甚至可以使用这个端点测试后端 API，甚至可以模拟一个集成的登录身份验证。 由于 Azure 静态 Web 应用程序支持这一点。 凉爽的！ 不是吗？

      使用 Vs Code 部署应用程序！

      要执行以下步骤，您需要在 GitHub 上分配您的项目并拥有一个免费的 Azure 帐户！

      现在让我们使用Visual Studio Code中的 Azure 静态 Web 应用程序扩展来部署这个应用程序！

      您可以在此处下载 VS Code 的 Azure 静态 Web 应用程序扩展

      现在在 Visual Studio Code 中打开项目，让我们按照以下步骤操作：

      步骤 01：

      单击+按钮，如下图所示：

      步骤02：

      您应该在此处定义应用程序名称。 名称必须是唯一的！

      步骤 03：

      由于 Astro 尚未在扩展中列出，让我们选择custom选项：

      步骤04：

      在这里，我们将定义应用程序的位置。 如果您的应用程序（项目根目录）位于某个子文件夹中（这是我的情况），请务必小心。 在这个例子中，在我的项目中，项目的根目录在astro-demo文件夹中

      步骤 05：

      最后，我们必须定义生成 Astro 工件文件的位置。 根据文档，它位于 dist 文件夹中（对于不同的框架，有不同的工件文件夹。要了解更多信息，请单击此处）

      步骤06：

      然后它将打开一个框，将您带到下一步（即 GitHub 存储库）

      步骤07：

      现在我们将被带到项目存储库网站，它将显示一个包含yaml文件的文件夹以及 GitHub 正在执行的操作。

      步骤08：

      现在有一个job操作在那里运行！ 这表明我们的应用程序正在与 Azure 平台进行通信，此外，还为我们创建了 CI 和 CD 传送带！

      步骤09：

      万一一切顺利！ 现在让我们进入最后一步！

      第 10 步：

      切换回 Visual Studio Code 并再次打开 SWA 扩展。 并且，寻找新创建的应用程序！ 右键单击： browser site

      现在打开浏览器！ 而且，您将看到您的项目在全球范围内可用并可供所有人查看！

      你可以看到一个在线版本：这里

      最后的话！

      当我们想要以某种方式部署静态应用程序时，Azure 静态 Web 应用程序可以为我们提供帮助和便利：简单、快速和动态！ 最好的部分 - 与 GitHub Actions 集成！ 从而为我们自动创建CI和CD的跑步机！ 太棒了，不是吗？！

      如果您想了解更多甚至免费试用此服务，我强烈推荐以下课程：

      要及时了解许多其他新闻，请务必在 Twitter 上关注我！

      拜拜！ 😍 😍



原文:

In May 2021 Azure Static Web Apps was released. A service that allows you to automatically deploy Full Stack applications (from the Back-End to the Front-End) completely free of charge on Azure from the GitHub repository.

The most interesting thing about Azure Static Web Apps is that this service integrates perfectly to host your application or static content globally and in a distributed way, on the Back-End side, creating API's using the Azure Functions service, including authentication resources, custom routing and even route-based authorization!
And best of all: hosting for free!

But, what is the Azure Static Web Apps CLI?!

Well, today's focus will be on a tool linked to this service that will allow you to locally emulate your static applications very quickly and dynamically: Azure Static Web Apps CLI!

The Static Web Apps CLI, also known as SWA CLI, serves as a local development tool for Azure Static Web Apps. It can:

  • Serve static app assets, or proxy to your app dev server
  • Serve API requests, or proxy to APIs running in Azure Functions Core Tools
  • Emulate authentication and authorization
  • Emulate Static Web Apps configuration, including routing

Static Web Apps CLI is in preview. If you have suggestions or you encounter issues, please report them or help us fix them. Your contributions are very much appreciated. 🙏

The CLI emulates commonly used capabilities of the Azure Static Web Apps cloud service. Some differences are expected. Always deploy and test your apps in Azure to confirm behavior.

Quickstart

Using npm or yarn:

  • Install the cli 
    npm install -g @azure/static-web-apps-cli
  • Open a SWA app folder at the root (outside any /api or /app…

According to the Azure Static Web Apps CLI tool repository with SWA CLI you can:

  • ✅ Serve static app assets, or proxy to your app dev server
  • ✅ Serve API requests, or proxy to APIs running in Azure Functions Core Tools
  • ✅ Emulate authentication and authorization
  • ✅ Emulate Static Web Apps configuration, including routing

Another important point to mention here is that you can emulate any static project or a Front-End application structure, whether it be:

  • ✅ React
  • ✅ Angular
  • ✅ Svelte
  • ✅ Vue
  • ✅ Next.js
  • ✅ Blazor/WebAssembly!

Fine! Talking is good! But it's so much better when we have DEMOS! So, let's code now and run a very simple demo of how we can use this tool locally!

Demo Time!

First thing we need to do is install Node.js! Always try to install the LTS version!

In this demo, I'll be using a new framework that could be a big trend in the development world: Astro Build.

I won't be going into details about this framework, because that's not the focus of this article. But if you would like to know more, you can watch the video below:

To speed up our demo a little more, I created a template project that I kindly ask you to download or git clone it:

git clone https://github.com/glaucia86/astro-swa-demo.git

If you wish, you can make the necessary modifications. Like, change images and other stuff! I highly recommend when you start a new Astro project, choose the option: Portfolio. You will have this same template to start with, maybe a new static portfolio site!

Go to the astro-demo folder and install the packages locally using the command:

Type the npm start command to start the project. Open the browser to the page: http://localhost:3000/

If you see it as shown in the image below, it's because everything worked just fine!

Now let's go to the next step! Open your command line and type the command:

npm install -g @azure/static-web-apps-cli

Create a folder so we can test this tool. In my case, I created a folder with the following name:

Now comes the coolest part, where just with the command line you can create an example static page. To do so, run the command below:

swa start http://localhost:3000 --run "npm start"

Now open the browser to http://localhost:4280/.

What does this mean to us? In fact, we are emulating the Azure Static Web Apps server. From this endpoint, we can even test Back-End APIs with this endpoint or even simulate an integrated login authentication. Since Azure Static Web Apps supports this. (More information HERE)

Cool! Isn't it?

Deploying the application using Vs Code!

To perform the steps below, you need to allocate your project on GitHub and have a free Azure account!

Now let's deploy this application using the Azure Static Web Apps extension in Visual Studio Code!

You can download the Azure Static Web Apps extension for VS Code HERE

Now open the project in Visual Studio Code and let's follow these steps:

Step 01:

Click in the + button as shown in the image below:

Step 02:

Here you should define the application name. The name must be unique!

Step 03:

As Astro is not yet listed in the extension, let's choose the custom option:

Step 04:

Here we will be defining the location of our application. Be very careful, if your application (project root) is in some subfolder (which is my case). In this example, from my project, the root of the project is in the astro-demo folder

Step 05:

And, finally, we have to define where the Astro artifact files are being generated. According to the documentation, it is located in the dist folder (for different frameworks there are different artifact folders. To learn more, click HERE)

Step 06:

Then it will open a box that will take you to the next step (i.e. the GitHub repository)

Step 07:

Now we will be taken to the project repository website, it will show a folder containing a yaml file and in addition, an action being executed by GitHub.

Step 08:

There is now a job action running there! This demonstrates that our application is communicating with the Azure platform and, in addition, creating a C.I and C.D belt for us!

Step 09:

In case everything works out! Now let's go to the last step!

Step 10:

Switch back to Visual Studio Code and open the SWA extension again. And, look for the newly created application! Right click on: browser site

Now open the browser! And, you will see your project available globally and ready for everyone to see!

You can see a online version: HERE

Final Words!

Azure Static Web Apps helps and facilitates us when we want to deploy static applications in a way: simple, fast and dynamic! And the best part about it all - integrated with GitHub Actions! Thus creating a treadmill of C.I and C.D automatically for us! Fantastic, isn't it?!

If you want to know more or even try this service for free, I highly recommend the courses below:

And to stay up to date with many other news, be sure to follow me on twitter!

See ya! 😍
Items.Author: glaucia86

Items.Title: 使用 Solidity、Node.js 和 Replit 构建智能合约预言机 | Build a smart contract oracle with Solidity, Node.js, and Replit
Items.Description:
预言机是运行在区块链上的智能合约协议和现实世界数据馈送之间的桥梁。 在之前的 Solidity 教程中，我们设计了一个简单的以太坊托管协议和一个可组合的机器人 NFT（第 1部分，第 2 部分）。 这些都是独立的系统。 在本教程中，我们将学习如何通过构建预言机将外部数据（例如汇率或随机数）提取到我们的智能合约中，以及如何组合不同的合约协议。

在本教程结束时，您将拥有：

  • 了解如何在智能合约中使用预言机以及预言机如何在内部工作。
  • 具有构建混合上链和链下系统的经验。
  • 具有编写合同协议的经验。

入门

我们需要两件事来开始这个项目：一个 Solidity repl 和一个浏览器钱包。

可靠性复制

如果您还没有登录，请登录Repli或创建一个帐户。 登录后，创建一个 Solidity starter repl。

与其每次我们想要测试一段新代码时都运行我们的 repl，我们可以运行我们的 repl 一次，以启动它，当我们在contract.sol中对我们的 Solidity 代码进行更改时，它会自动重新加载。

我们将部署到 Replit 测试网，这是由 Replit 管理并针对测试进行优化的以太坊区块链的自定义版本。

浏览器钱包

我们将需要一个基于浏览器的 Web3 钱包来与 Replit 测试网和我们部署的合约进行交互。 您可以从MetaMask 的下载页面安装它。 确保您使用的是受支持的浏览器——Chrome、Firefox、Brave 或 Edge。

安装 MetaMask 后，按照提示创建钱包并登录。MetaMask 会给你一个 12 字的秘密恢复短语——这是你钱包的私钥，必须保密。 如果您丢失了此短语，您将无法访问您的钱包。 如果其他人找到它，他们会的。

如果您已经在使用 MetaMask，我们建议您创建一个新帐户以使用 Replit 进行测试。 您可以从帐户菜单中执行此操作，该菜单在您单击 MetaMask 界面右上角的帐户头像时出现。

甲骨文设计

预言机是一个混合系统，由合约和传统的 Web 服务器代码组成。 合约为其他合约请求和接收数据提供接口，Web 服务器代码使用事件和合约函数来响应这些请求并提供所需的数据。 在高层次上，架构如下所示：

用户与不同的智能合约协议进行交互，例如去中心化交易所或 NFT 市场。 这些协议可以从 oracle 智能合约获取数据，该合约从链下数据提供者（这些通常是某种形式的 API）接收数据。

在本教程中，我们将使用RANDOM.ORG API构建一个用于随机数生成的预言机。 如果您完成了我们的ReplBot NFT 教程，您就会知道在区块链上几乎不可能获得真正的随机性，因此预言机确实是需要随机数的代码的唯一解决方案。

在有关以太坊预言机的大量讨论和文档中，“预言机”一词可互换使用，指代三种不同的事物：

  1. 链下数据提供者
  2. 将数据连接到区块链上的 Oracle 合约
  3. 由 1 和 2 组成的完整解决方案

为避免混淆，我们将在本教程中使用以下术语：

  1. 提供者
  2. 甲骨文合同
  3. 神谕

调用者合约

我们将首先假设我们的预言机已经构建，并开发一个将从它请求随机数的合约。 这听起来像是本末倒置，但以这种方式开发将使我们清楚地了解我们想要从成品中得到什么以及它必须如何工作。

这份合约，我们将其命名为Caller ，将非常简单。 它要做的就是允许用户请求随机数并在events中发出这些数字。 在本教程结束时，您可以扩展Caller以对数字做一些更有趣的事情。

我们将使用 Chainlink 的基本请求模型作为基础来设计我们的预言机。 由于从预言机获取数据需要链下交互，我们将无法通过单个函数调用获取随机数。 相反，我们将实现一个函数来请求一个随机数，该随机数将由合约的用户调用，以及第二个函数来满足一个请求，该请求将由预言机合约调用。 这与 JavaScript 中的回调类似。

在您的 repl 中创建一个名为contracts的新目录。 然后在contracts/caller创建一个子目录。 在此子目录中，创建一个名为Caller.sol的新文件。 在新文件中输入以下代码：

 // SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT pragma solidity ^ 0.8 . 2 ; import "@openzeppelin-solidity/contracts/access/Ownable.sol" ; import "./IRandOracle.sol" ; contract Caller is Ownable { }

此Caller合约存根导入两个依赖项：

  • 名为IRandOracle的本地合约。 这是一个接口，告诉该合约如何与预言机合约进行交互。

在我们填写Caller的逻辑之前，让我们创建那个接口。 在同一目录中创建一个名为IRandOracle.sol的新文件，并向其中添加以下代码：

 // SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT pragma solidity ^ 0.8 . 2 ; interface IRandOracle { function requestRandomNumber () external returns ( uint256 ); }

就是这样！ 接口不包含任何实现细节，甚至不必在它们引用的合约中指定每个外部函数。 由于Caller只会调用这一个预言机合约函数，因此这是我们唯一需要指定的。

现在让我们回到Caller.sol并实现一些逻辑。 在合约的开始和结束大括号之间添加以下代码：

 IRandOracle private randOracle ; mapping ( uint256 => bool ) requests ; mapping ( uint256 => uint256 ) results ;

我们首先创建一个变量来引用我们的预言机合约，然后创建两个映射：

  • 然后我们可以添加一些家政功能：

     modifier onlyRandOracle () { require ( msg . sender == address ( randOracle ), "Unauthorized." ); _ ; } function setRandOracleAddress ( address newAddress ) external onlyOwner { randOracle = IRandOracle ( newAddress ); emit OracleAddressChanged ( newAddress ); }

    首先，我们定义onlyRandOracle修饰符，我们将使用它来限制对我们的实现函数的访问。 如果函数调用者的地址不是预言机合约的地址，它通过使用require语句抛出错误来做到这一点。 没有它，任何用户都可以提交他们选择的“随机”数字来满足我们的要求。

    其次，我们添加一个onlyOwner函数（这是另一个修饰符，在 OpenZeppelin 的Ownable中定义）来设置我们将使用的预言机合约的地址。 作为合约所有者，我们将能够在必要时更改预言机地址。

    我们的代码使用提供的地址创建我们的IRandOracle接口的实例，然后发出一个事件让用户知道对合约进行了更改。 编写良好的合约应该为这样的配置更改发出事件，以便它们的操作对用户保持透明。

    完成内务处理后，我们现在可以在setRandOracleAddress()的定义下编写Caller的主要函数。 首先， getRandomNumber() ：

     function getRandomNumber () external { require ( randOracle != IRandOracle ( address ( 0 )), "Oracle not initialized." ); uint256 id = randOracle . requestRandomNumber (); requests [ id ] = true ; emit RandomNumberRequested ( id ); }

    这里我们使用require语句来确保合约的预言机被初始化。 我们通过检查它是否不是位于空地址的合约来做到这一点，空地址是未初始化合约引用的地址。 然后我们调用requestRandomNumber() ，我们在IRandOracle接口中声明的函数。 此函数将返回一个请求 ID，我们在requests映射中将其标记为有效。 最后，我们发出一个事件来表明已经请求了一个随机数。

    现在我们需要编写回调函数。 在上面添加的函数下方添加以下代码：

     function fulfillRandomNumberRequest ( uint256 randomNumber , uint256 id ) external onlyRandOracle { require ( requests [ id ], "Request is invalid or already fulfilled." ); results [ id ] = randomNumber ; delete requests [ id ]; emit RandomNumberReceived ( randomNumber , id ); }

    当 oracle 合约调用此函数时（根据onlyRandOracle ，只有它被允许这样做），它将提供请求的随机数以及它正在执行的请求 ID。 该函数将首先检查请求 ID 是否有效，然后将随机数存储在results映射中。

    现在请求已经完成，它还会从requests中delete请求 ID，相当于将其设置为false 。 这将确保仅跟踪活动请求。

    最后，我们的回调函数会发出一个事件来宣布请求已完成。 在更复杂的合约中，此函数不仅仅将随机数存储在结果映射中：例如，它可能使用该数字来确定彩票中奖者，或生成 NFT 的属性。

    在我们结束这个合约之前，我们需要定义我们在上面发出的事件。 将以下代码添加到合约正文的底部：

     event OracleAddressChanged ( address oracleAddress ); event RandomNumberRequested ( uint256 id ); event RandomNumberReceived ( uint256 number , uint256 id );

    我们的调用者合约现在已经完成。 但在我们实施它所依赖的预言机合约之前，它无法做任何事情。

    甲骨文合约

    如果您再看一下上面的架构图，您会注意到预言机合约旨在与多个链下数据提供者进行交互。 这是为了确保去中心化，这是强大的智能合约协议的一个关键属性。 如果我们依赖单个数据提供者来提供随机数，那么如果该提供者遭到破坏并且它发送给我们的数字开始被修复，或者如果它出现中断并停止返回任何东西，我们就会遇到麻烦。

    因此，为了最大程度地减少任何单个提供程序受到损害或出现故障的影响，我们将实现从多个不同提供程序中获取多个不同随机数的功能，最后我们将使用按位 XOR组合这些随机数。

    在您的 repl 中的contracts/oracle中创建一个新子目录。 在此子目录中，创建一个名为RandOracle.sol的新文件。 在新文件中输入以下代码：

     // SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT pragma solidity ^ 0.8 . 2 ; import "@openzeppelin/contracts/access/AccessControl.sol" ; import "./ICaller.sol" ; contract RandOracle is AccessControl { }

    这个存根与我们在编写Caller时开始使用的代码非常相似，只有两个关键区别：

    • 我们从 OpenZeppelin 导入AccessControl而不是Ownable ，这将允许我们实现基于角色的访问控制，这是一个比我们在Caller中使用的更复杂和更精细的授权模型。 虽然我们也可以将它用于Caller ，但这会有点过头了，我们希望让我们的合约尽可能小，以节省部署成本。
    • 我们导入接口ICaller IRandOracle 您可能会猜到它将包含什么。 现在让我们在同一目录中名为ICaller.sol的文件中创建它：
     // SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT pragma solidity ^ 0.8 . 2 ; interface ICaller { function fulfillRandomNumberRequest ( uint256 randomNumber , uint256 id ) external ; }

    像Caller一样， RandOracle只需要知道另一个合约中的一个函数。

    让我们回到RandOracle.sol并定义一些状态变量。

     bytes32 public constant PROVIDER_ROLE = keccak256 ( "PROVIDER_ROLE" );

    首先，我们根据AccessControl合同的文档为我们的数据提供者角色定义一个名称。 然后我们定义两个变量，我们将使用它们来管理多个提供者：

     uint private numProviders = 0 ; uint private providersThreshold = 1 ;

    我们使用numProviders来存储我们添加到合约中的数据提供者的总数，并使用providersThreshold来定义我们需要考虑满足请求的提供者响应的最小数量。 目前，我们只将providersThreshold设置为一个，从而使我们面临上述集中化风险，但这足以启动和运行一个基本的演示。

    接下来，我们需要定义一些用于处理请求和响应的变量。 在上面刚刚添加的定义下方输入以下代码：

     uint private randNonce = 0 ; mapping ( uint256 => bool ) private pendingRequests ; struct Response { address providerAddress ; address callerAddress ; uint256 randomNumber ; } mapping ( uint256 => Response []) private idToResponses ;

    这里我们定义：

    • 这将是一个简单的计数器，我们每次调用requestRandomNumber()时都会递增。
    • 这将允许我们跟踪每个请求的响应。

    现在让我们定义合约的构造函数，即在部署时将运行的函数。

     constructor () { _setupRole ( DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE , msg . sender ); // make the deployer admin }

    该函数将AccessControl的DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE分配给合约的部署地址，通常称为其所有者。 该角色有权授予和撤销其他角色。

    现在我们准备定义requestRandomNumber() ，我们从Caller的函数。 在构造函数的定义下面添加以下代码：

     function requestRandomNumber () external returns ( uint256 ) { require ( numProviders > 0 , " No data providers not yet added." ); randNonce ++ ; uint id = uint ( keccak256 ( abi . encodePacked ( block . timestamp , msg . sender , randNonce ))) % 1000 ; pendingRequests [ id ] = true ; emit RandomNumberRequested ( msg . sender , id ); return id ; }

    这段代码所做的只是根据randNonce为请求生成一个唯一 ID，将该 ID 添加到pendingRequests中，发出一个事件并返回该 ID，类似于支持台票务系统的概念。 如果合约的管理员尚未添加任何数据提供者，则代码顶部的require语句将恢复。

    我们稍后将使用 Node.js 创建一个链下数据提供程序，它将监视合约中的RandomNumberRequested事件，并通过向合约函数returnRandomNumber()提交一个随机数来响应它们，该函数又将返回该数字使用fulfillRandomNumberRequest()到调用者合约。 现在让我们开始编写returnRandomNumber() ，就在requestRandomNumber()的下面：

     function returnRandomNumber ( uint256 randomNumber , address callerAddress , uint256 id ) external onlyRole ( PROVIDER_ROLE ) { require ( pendingRequests [ id ], "Request not found." ); // Add newest response to list Response memory res = Response ( msg . sender , callerAddress , randomNumber ); idToResponses [ id ]. push ( res ); uint numResponses = idToResponses [ id ]. length ; }

    在这里， returnRandomNumber是一个公共函数，我们将使用PROVIDER_ROLE限制地址。 它需要三个参数：随机数、发出初始请求的地址和请求 ID。 在上面的代码中，我们确保请求 ID 有效，然后将响应添加到具有此 ID 的请求的响应数组中，存储在idToResponses中。 然后我们将数组的长度存储在numResponses中，我们将使用它来检查我们是否达到了响应阈值。

    在函数的下一部分中，我们将合并我们收到的所有响应并将结果提交回调用者。 在以uint numResponses开头的行下方添加以下代码：

     // Check if we've received enough responses if ( numResponses == providersThreshold ) { uint compositeRandomNumber = 0 ; // Loop through the array and combine responses for ( uint i = 0 ; i < idToResponses [ id ]. length ; i ++ ) { compositeRandomNumber = compositeRandomNumber ^ idToResponses [ id ][ i ]. randomNumber ; // bitwise XOR } // Clean up delete pendingRequests [ id ]; delete idToResponses [ id ]; // Fulfill request ICaller ( callerAddress ). fulfillRandomNumberRequest ( compositeRandomNumber , id ); emit RandomNumberReturned ( compositeRandomNumber , callerAddress , id ); }

    此if块中的代码仅在最终数据提供者提交其随机数时运行。 它通过按位 XOR组合所有随机数，因为取平均值会降低随机性。 然后它会删除这个请求的数据——如果没有这个步骤，数据提供者可以继续提交不同的随机数并改变结果。 然后它通过调用调用者函数的回调来满足请求，最后发出一个事件。

    在执行了所有状态更改操作之后，几乎总是应该在给定函数的末尾或附近调用外部合约中的函数。 这避免了重入的风险，这是一个常见的智能合约错误，是著名的2016 年 DAO 黑客攻击的原因。

    现在已经完成了我们的预言机合约的主要功能，我们需要为合约管理员添加一些管家功能来管理数据提供者。 我们将从添加新数据提供者的函数开始。 在合约正文的底部添加以下代码：

     // Admin functions function addProvider ( address provider ) external onlyRole ( DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE ) { require ( ! hasRole ( PROVIDER_ROLE , provider ), "Provider already added." ); _grantRole ( PROVIDER_ROLE , provider ); numProviders ++ ; emit ProviderAdded ( provider ); }

    这段代码非常简单。 在重复检查之后，它使用AccessControl中的_grantRole()函数将PROVIDER_ROLE分配给指定的地址，递增numProviders并发出一个事件以让用户知道合约配置更改。

    请注意，我们使用了internal函数_grantRole()而不是public函数grantRole() ：我们可以这样做，因为internal函数可以在同一个合约以及从它继承的所有合约中使用。 这与不能在子合同中调用的private函数不同。

    接下来，我们添加一个函数来删除数据提供者：

     function removeProvider ( address provider ) external onlyRole ( DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE ) { require ( ! hasRole ( PROVIDER_ROLE , provider ), "Address is not a recognized provider." ); require ( numProviders > 1 , "Cannot remove the only provider." ); _revokeRole ( PROVIDER_ROLE , provider ); numProviders -- ; emit ProviderRemoved ( provider ); }

    此函数执行上述函数的反向操作，并进行额外检查以确保管理员不会从合约中删除所有提供者，从而使其无法使用。

    我们需要的最后一个管理功能是设置提供者阈值的功能。 在removeProvider()的主体下方添加以下代码：

     function setProvidersThreshold ( uint threshold ) external onlyRole ( DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE ) { require ( threshold > 0 , "Threshold cannot be zero." ); providersThreshold = threshold ; emit ProvidersThresholdChanged ( providersThreshold ); }

    最后，让我们定义上面发出的事件。 将以下代码添加到合约正文的底部：

     // Events event RandomNumberRequested ( address callerAddress , uint id ); event RandomNumberReturned ( uint256 randomNumber , address callerAddress , uint id ); event ProviderAdded ( address providerAddress ); event ProviderRemoved ( address providerAddress ); event ProvidersThresholdChanged ( uint threshold );

    我们的预言机合约已经完成。 接下来，我们需要创建一个数据提供者来为其提供随机数。

    我们将把我们的数据提供者编写为一个简单的 Node.js 应用程序，使用Hardhat Ethereum 开发环境来帮助我们与区块链进行交互。 该环境为编写、测试和部署智能合约以及构建与区块链接口的应用程序提供了许多功能，例如这个。 它包括稍加修改的ethers库版本，这是我们将使用的主要组件。

    如前所述，我们的数据提供者将监控预言机合约中的随机数请求并提交响应。 由于 JavaScript 程序是单线程的，我们将使用分块队列结构来分割程序在记录新请求和处理现有请求之间的时间。

    在开始开发之前，我们需要安装 Hardhat。 打开你的 repl 的package.json文件，在dependencies项对象的底部添加以下行（记得在前一行的末尾添加一个逗号）：

    在您的 repl 中创建一个名为provider的新目录。 在该目录中，创建一个名为index.js的 JavaScript 文件。 将以下初始代码添加到文件中：

     const hardhat = require ( ' hardhat ' ); const axios = require ( ' axios ' ); const MAX_RETRIES = 5 ; const SLEEP_TIME = 2000 ; const BATCH_SIZE = 3 ;

    这里我们导入hardhat和axios ，我们需要从RANDOM.ORG API 请求随机数。

    我们定义的常量将按如下方式使用：

    • 我们满足请求的能力可能会受到 RANDOM.ORG 中断的影响，或者我们的数据提供商的钱包没有足够的以太坊来支付执行returnRandomNumber()的 gas 成本。
    • 在深入研究应用程序的主要代码之前，让我们创建一个从 RANDOM.ORG 获取随机数的函数。 在常量定义下方添加以下代码：

       async function requestRandomNumber () { const res = await axios ({ url : ' https://www.random.org/integers/ ' , params : { num : 1 , min : 1 , max : 1000 , col : 1 , base : 10 , format : ' plain ' , rnd : ' new ' }, method : ' get ' }); return parseInt ( res . data ); }

      在这里，我们使用axios构造并向 RANDOM.ORG 的整数生成器提交 HTTP 请求，按照他们的文档。 由于axios是一个异步函数，我们必须使用await关键字来接收它的最终返回值。 我们从 ethers 库中使用的大多数函数也是如此。

      现在让我们从应用程序的内容开始。 将以下代码添加到文件底部：

       async function main () { // Initialize account const [ dataProvider ] = await hardhat . ethers . getSigners (); // Initialize contract const oracleContractAddress = " ORACLE-CONTRACT-ADDRESS-HERE " ; const oracleContractABI = require ( " ./randOracleABI.json " ); const oracleContract = new hardhat . ethers . Contract ( oracleContractAddress , oracleContractABI , dataProvider ); } main ();

      对于前端 dapp，这通常是像 MetaMask 这样的钱包扩展，但对于像这样的脚本，我们需要指定一个私钥。 我们稍后会这样做，当我们将所有内容组合在一起时。

      下一段代码初始化一个Contract对象。 要连接到合约并运行其功能，我们需要两件事：合约的地址，它告诉我们在哪里可以找到它，以及它的应用程序二进制接口（ABI），它告诉我们它实现了哪些功能以及它们的参数是什么。 我们暂时将它们保留为占位符。

      请注意，我们还将dataProvider指定为Contract对象的构造函数的参数。 这提供了调用合约函数时使用的默认帐户。

      完成该设置后，我们可以开始观察请求并填充我们的队列。 将以下代码添加到main()函数的底部：

       // Populate requests queue var requestsQueue = []; oracleContract . on ( " RandomNumberRequested " , async ( callerAddress , id ) => { requestsQueue . push ({ callerAddress , id }); });

      在这里，我们初始化了一个空请求队列并使用oracleContract的on方法订阅RandomNumberRequested事件。 每当我们的合约发出这种类型的新事件时，我们的回调中的代码将执行，将一个包含事件参数的对象附加到requestsQueue 。

      这是我们功能的输入部分，所以现在我们需要输出。 如上所述，我们将定期以块的形式处理队列，因此让我们将下一段代码包装在 [ setInterval() ] 函数中。 将以下代码添加到main()函数的底部：

       // Poll and process requests queue at intervals setInterval ( async () => { let processedRequests = 0 ; }, SLEEP_TIME );

      我们使用processedRequests来跟踪我们在这个周期中处理了多少请求，最多BATCH_SIZE 。 让我们从一个简单的while循环开始，使用shift()方法将第一个元素从请求队列中弹出。 在setInterval主体中的processedRequests请求定义下方添加以下代码：

       while ( requestsQueue . length > 0 && processedRequests < BATCH_SIZE ) { const request = requestsQueue . shift (); }

      现在我们可以使用下一段代码来处理请求，您可以将其添加到while循环中，在request的定义下方：

       let retries = 0 ; while ( retries < MAX_RETRIES ) { try { const randomNumber = await requestRandomNumber (); await oracleContract . returnRandomNumber ( randomNumber , request . callerAddress , request . id ); break ; } catch ( error ) { retries ++ ; } } processedRequests ++ ;

      我们使用try... catch来顺利处理发生的任何错误。 我们希望能够让这个程序继续运行，而不用担心它会因错误而崩溃。

      首先，此代码尝试请求一个随机数，并通过调用returnRandomNumber()将其发送到我们的预言机合约。 如果操作成功，我们会跳出重试循环并继续下一个请求。 如果不是，我们增加retries计数器并重试。

      如果达到最大重试次数，我们将转到下一个请求，而不向 oracle 合约提交任何内容。

      这就是我们的数据提供者。 在我们将它们放在一起之前，我们需要编写的最后一件事是我们的Caller合约的简单前端，为人们请求和接收随机数提供了一种用户友好的方式。

      调用者合约前端

      在 repl 中创建一个名为frontend的新文件夹，然后在其中创建两个文件： index.html和app.js 。 将以下标记添加到frontend/index.html ：

       <!DOCTYPE html> <html> <head> <title> Random Oracle Client </title> <style> .request { color : red ; } .response { color : green ; } </style> <head> <body> <button id= "request-rand" class= "button" > Request random number </button><br> <pre id= "request-id" ></pre> <ul id= "events" ></ul> <script src= "https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/web3@latest/dist/web3.min.js" ></script> <script src= "./callerABI.js" ></script> <script src= "./app.js" ></script> </body> </html>

      在此文件中，我们创建了以下内容：

      • 用于请求新随机数的button元素。
      • 一个pre元素，我们将在其中打印最后一个请求的 ID。
      • 用于在合约事件发生时记录它们的ul元素。

      我们还在文件顶部链接了样式表，并在底部链接了以下 JavaScript 文件：

      • 我们的app.js文件，它将包含前端的逻辑。

      打开frontend/app.js ，进入代码骨架：

       App = { callerAddress : " FILL-ME-IN " , callerContract : null , init : async function () {}, } App . init ();

      这个App对象将包含我们将实现的所有状态和功能。 请注意，此前端将仅与Caller交互，而不与RandOracle 。 部署完所有内容后，我们将返回并在下一部分中输入合约地址。

      按如下方式填充您的空函数体：

       init : async function () { if ( window . ethereum ) { await window . ethereum . request ({ method : ' eth_requestAccounts ' }); window . web3 = new Web3 ( window . ethereum ); App . callerContract = new web3 . eth . Contract ( callerABI , callerAddress ); // Switch networks App . switchToReplitTestnet (); } },

      此代码将与 MetaMask 交互并显示一个弹出窗口，要求用户将他们的钱包连接到我们的 dapp。 如果用户接受，我们将使用他们的帐户详细信息实例化一个Web3对象。 与 ethers 一样，Web3 的大部分功能都是异步的。

      因为我们是在 Replit Testnet 上构建的，所以我们需要提示用户切换到这个网络，我们将在函数switchToReplitTestnet()中执行此操作。 在init的定义下方添加此函数的定义：

       switchToReplitTestnet : function () { window . ethereum . request ({ method : " wallet_addEthereumChain " , params : [ { chainId : " 0x7265706c " , chainName : " Replit Testnet " , rpcUrls : [ " https://eth.replit.com " ], iconUrls : [ " https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/b2/Repl.it_logo.svg " , ], nativeCurrency : { name : " Replit ETH " , symbol : " RΞ " , decimals : 18 , }, }, ], }); },

      此代码提供 MetaMask 提示用户切换网络所需的详细信息。

      设置完成，我们可以继续使用应用程序功能。 我们将从调用Caller合约的getRandomNumber()函数的函数开始。

       // contract interactions getRandomNumber : async function () { const accounts = await web3 . eth . getAccounts (); const account = accounts [ 0 ]; // Request random number & return request ID return ( await App . callerContract . methods . getRandomNumber (). send ({ from : account })); },

      这里我们使用web3.eth.getAccounts()来查找用户的钱包地址，我们使用它来调用合约函数getRandomNumber() 。 请注意，Web3 中调用函数的模式和语法与我们在 ethers 中使用的语法有很大不同。

      接下来我们需要一个函数来订阅合约事件并将它们的详细信息添加到我们网页的事件日志中。 添加以下代码：

       subscribeToContractEvents : function () { App . callerContract . events . RandomNumberRequested ( async ( err , event ) => { if ( err ) console . error ( ' Error on event ' , err ) // Create list item let reqEventLi = document . createElement ( " li " ); reqEventLi . classList . add ( " request " ); reqEventLi . innerHTML = `Random number requested, ID: ${ event . returnValues . id } ` ; // Add to top of list const eventLog = document . getElementById ( " events " ); eventLog . prepend ( reqEventLi ); }); App . callerContract . events . RandomNumberReceived ( async ( err , event ) => { if ( err ) console . error ( ' Error on event ' , err ) // Create list item let recEventLi = document . createElement ( " li " ); recEventLi . classList . add ( " response " ); recEventLi . innerHTML = `Random number received for ID ${ event . returnValues . id } : ${ event . returnValues . number } ` ; // Add to top of list const eventLog = document . getElementById ( " events " ); eventLog . prepend ( recEventLi ); }); },

      此代码类似于我们在数据提供程序中使用的事件订阅代码，但使用web3.js Contract.events属性。 它等待新的合约事件被发出，并将每个事件的记录添加到页面事件列表的顶部。

      接下来我们需要处理 JavaScript 事件并将我们的随机数字按钮绑定到我们上面编写的合约交互函数。 添加以下代码：

       // interface bindBrowserEvents : function () { const requestButton = document . getElementById ( " request-rand " ); requestButton . addEventListener ( " click " , async function () { const transaction = await App . getRandomNumber (); const requestID = document . getElementById ( " request-id " ); requestID . innerHTML = `Submitted! Request ID: ${ transaction . events . RandomNumberRequested . returnValues . id } ` ; }); },

      这段代码将确保当我们点击按钮时，合约函数将被调用，并且其发出的事件中的请求 ID 将被打印到页面上。

      在我们打包这个文件之前，我们需要在我们的init()函数中调用这两个与事件相关的函数。 修改函数如下：

       init : async function () { if ( window . ethereum ) { await window . ethereum . request ({ method : ' eth_requestAccounts ' }); window . web3 = new Web3 ( window . ethereum ); App . callerContract = new web3 . eth . Contract ( callerABI , App . callerAddress ); // Switch networks App . switchToReplitTestnet (); } // NEW CODE BELOW App . subscribeToContractEvents (); App . bindBrowserEvents (); },

      我们现在已经拥有了启动和运行 oracle 和 oracle 客户端所需的一切。 在下一节中，我们将部署我们的合约并连接我们构建的所有内容。

      把它们放在一起

      我们在上面的部分中创建了很多不同的部分。 现在是拼拼拼图的时候了。

      编译和部署合约

      我们将从编译和部署我们的合约开始。 但首先，我们需要更改我们的依赖项。 我们的 oracle 合约依赖于 OpenZeppelin 4.6.0 版本的代码，因此我们需要编辑我们的 repl 的package.json以确保我们导入该版本。 现在打开package.json ，找到依赖@openzeppelin/contracts并将其旁边的版本号更改为^4.6.0 。

      要在 Solidity 入门存储库中编译我们的合约，我们需要通过完整路径将它们导入到contract.sol中。 删除contract.sol的当前内容并将其替换为以下内容：

       // SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT pragma solidity ^ 0.8 . 2 ; import "/home/runner/YOUR-REPL-NAME-HERE/contracts/caller/Caller.sol" ; import "/home/runner/YOUR-REPL-NAME-HERE/contracts/oracle/RandOracle.sol" ;

      按照指示输入您的 repl 名称。 然后运行您的 repl 并等待 Solidity 启动器 Web 界面出现，并等待两个合约编译。

      编译完成后，我们可以部署，但为此我们需要一些资金。 将您的 MetaMask 钱包连接到 Web 界面并切换到 Replit 测试网。 然后点击链接获取 1 ETH 用于测试。 等到 1 ETH 出现在页面右上角的钱包余额中。


      现在你可以部署你的合约了。 从下拉框中选择“调用者”，然后单击“部署”。 批准出现的 MetaMask 弹出窗口。 然后对“RandOracle”做同样的事情。

      部署两个合约后，它们将显示为下拉框下方的可扩展框。 展开它们并查看每个可用的功能。

      在实践中， Caller和RandOracle合约通常会部署在不同的地址，可能属于完全不同的团队，但我们使用一个单独的地址来避免一遍又一遍地登录和退出 MetaMask。

      初始化合约

      接下来，我们需要将Caller连接到RandOracle 。 在Caller中找到setRandOracleAddress函数。 然后单击RandOracle下拉框底部的地址，将其复制并粘贴为newAddress的值。 然后单击运行。

      我们已经完成了合约的设置，但是让你的 repl 在 Solidity 启动 Web 界面打开的情况下运行，因为我们需要它来设置我们的数据提供者应用程序和客户端前端。

      准备数据提供者

      在我们将数据提供者与区块链集成之前，让我们在 repl 的package.json文件中包含axios 。 如果axios尚未作为依赖项包含，则将以下行添加到dependencies项对象的底部（请记住在前一行的末尾添加逗号）：

      完成后，我们需要将RandOracle的 ABI 复制到数据提供者可以看到的文件中。 单击RandOracle地址旁边的Copy ABI 将 ABI加载到剪贴板中。 然后在provider中创建一个名为randOracleABI.json的新文件，并将剪贴板的内容粘贴到其中。

      现在打开index.js ，找到以const oracleContractAddress开头的行，并将占位符字符串替换为已部署的RandOracle合约的地址。

      为了让我们的数据提供者正常工作，我们需要做的最后一件事是为其提供一个钱包。 我们用于 MetaMask 的钱包是由密码锁定的，因此不能轻易用于自动化操作。 无论如何，为我们的数据提供者创建一个专用钱包对我们来说更卫生。 我们将使用一个简单的 Node.js 脚本和ethereumjs-wallet库来做到这一点。

      打开 repl 的package.json文件。 将以下行添加到dependencies项对象的底部（请记住在前一行的末尾添加逗号）：

       "ethereumjs-wallet" : "^1.0.2"

      现在创建一个名为walletGen.js的新文件。 使用以下代码填充它：

       const wallet = require ( ' ethereumjs-wallet ' ). default ; const addressData = wallet . generate (); console . log ( `Private key: ${ addressData . getPrivateKeyString ()} ` ); console . log ( `Address: ${ addressData . getAddressString ()} ` );

      停止并运行您的 repl。 导航到右下角面板中的 Shell 选项卡并运行命令node walletGen.js 。 您应该会在命令下方看到两个长长的字母和数字字符串。 第一个是你新钱包的私钥，第二个是你新钱包的地址。

      任何知道这个私钥的人都可以控制钱包，所以我们需要保证它的安全。 打开 repl 侧边栏上的 Secrets 选项卡。 创建名为DATAPROVIDER_PRIVATE_KEY的新密钥并将私钥粘贴为其值。 单击“添加新值”按钮以保存条目。

      现在我们需要添加新钱包的地址作为我们的RandOracle合约的数据提供者。 在您的 repl 的 Web 浏览器中，导航到RandOracle中的addProvider函数。 粘贴新钱包的地址作为provider的值。 然后单击运行。

      我们的新钱包还需要一些 ETH 来向区块链发送随机数响应。 我们可以使用 MetaMask 发送一些我们的。 打开 MetaMask 扩展界面，点击标记为Send的按钮。 它应该在您的天平下方。 将数据提供者的钱包地址粘贴到出现的搜索栏中。 然后在Amount字段中输入一个小数字，例如 0.2，点击Next ，并确认交易。

      现在我们需要配置 Hardhat，以便它知道在运行我们的数据提供者应用程序时要连接到哪个网络（Replit 测试网）和使用哪个钱包（我们刚刚创建的那个）。 首先将以下行添加到dependencies项对象的底部（记得在上一行的末尾添加逗号）：

       "@nomiclabs/hardhat-waffle" : "^2.0.3"

      创建一个名为hardhat.config.js的文件，其内容如下：

       require ( " @nomiclabs/hardhat-waffle " ); // You need to export an object to set up your config // Go to https://hardhat.org/config/ to learn more /** * @type import('hardhat/config').HardhatUserConfig */ module . exports = { networks : { replit : { url : `https://eth.replit.com` , accounts : [ process . env . DATAPROVIDER_PRIVATE_KEY ] } }, solidity : " 0.8.2 " }

      这就是我们的数据提供者。 现在我们只需要准备客户端前端。

      准备客户端前端

      我们需要填写Caller的 ABI 和地址。 单击Caller地址旁边的Copy ABI以将 ABI 加载到剪贴板中。 然后在frontend创建一个名为callerABI.js的新文件，并在其中添加以下代码：

      在等号之后粘贴剪贴板的内容。 我们已经在frontend/index.html中包含了这个文件，所以现在我们只需要合约地址。 打开frontend/app.js并将Caller的地址粘贴到文件顶部附近的callerAddress占位符值的位置。

      我们还需要创建一个简单的Node.js Express应用程序来呈现前端。 在您的 repl 中创建一个名为frontend.js的文件，并向其中添加以下代码：

       const express = require ( ' express ' ); const app = express (); const PORT = 433 ; app . use ( express . static ( ' frontend ' )); app . listen ( PORT , () => console . log ( `Server listening on port: ${ PORT } ` ));

      运行 repl

      现在我们已经连接了所有东西，我们想用我们的数据提供者应用程序和客户端前端替换 Solidity 启动界面。 为此，请确保显示隐藏文件并打开.replit 。

      将run = "node tools"替换为以下内容：

      这将使我们的 repl 运行一个Bash 脚本而不是单个命令，这在我们需要运行多个东西时很有用。 创建一个名为start.sh的文件并向其中添加以下代码：

       # Start data provider in the background npx hardhat run provider --network replit & # Start client frontend node frontend.js

      我们必须将提供程序作为 Hardhat 脚本而不是普通的 Node.js 应用程序运行才能使用我们的 Hardhat 配置。 我们的客户端和预言机终于都准备好了。 停止您的 repl 并重新运行它。 您应该在 repl 浏览器中看到我们的前端。 单击按钮以请求随机数并观看事件。

      下一步去哪儿？

      在本教程中，我们构建了Chainlink 的可验证随机函数预言机的简化版本。 虽然我们没有提供相同级别的强大安全性，但构建它让我们深入了解智能合约如何处理链下数据，我们可以在未来将其用于其他项目，或者让其他 Solidity 开发人员在 Replit将其用于他们的项目。

      您可能想尝试的一些后续步骤：

      • 创建一些仅包含数据提供者代码的新副本，并为每个副本提供自己的钱包。 将地址作为数据提供者添加到RandOracle ，提高提供者阈值，并查看合同如何与多个提供者一起使用。
      • 在Client之上构建更复杂的东西。 例如，您可以调整ReplBot NFT 合约以将此预言用作随机源。 或者你可以写一些类似彩票合同的东西。
      • 尝试不同的数据源。 预言机可以成为您能想到的任何数据的来源，从股票价格到天气。

      你可以在这里找到我们的repl。



原文:

Oracles are bridges between smart contract protocols running on the blockchain and real-world data feeds. In previous Solidity tutorials, we've designed a simple Ethereum escrow protocol and a composable robot NFT (Part 1, Part 2). These were both self-contained systems. In this tutorial, we'll learn about how to pull external data (such as exchange rates or random numbers) into our smart contracts by building an oracle, as well as how to combine different contract protocols.

By the end of this tutorial, you will have:

  • An understanding of how to use oracles in smart contracts and how oracles work internally.
  • Experience with building a hybrid on-and-off chain system.
  • Experience with composing contract protocols.

Getting started

We need two things to get started with this project: a Solidity repl and a browser wallet.

Solidity repl

Sign in to Replit or create an account if you haven't already. Once logged in, create a Solidity starter repl.

The Solidity starter repl works a little differently from other repls you may have used in the past. Rather than running our repl every time we want to test out a new piece of code, we can run our repl once, to start it up, and it will automatically reload when changes are made to our Solidity code in contract.sol.

The Solidity starter repl comes with a friendly web interface, built using the web3 Ethereum JavaScript API, which we will use to deploy and interact with our contracts. We will deploy to Replit Testnet, a custom version of the Ethereum blockchain managed by Replit and optimised for testing.

Browser wallet

We will need a browser-based Web3 wallet to interact with the Replit Testnet and our deployed contracts. MetaMask is a popular and feature-rich wallet implemented as a WebExtension. You can install it from MetaMask's download page. Make sure you're using a supported browser – Chrome, Firefox, Brave, or Edge.

Once you've installed MetaMask, follow the prompts to create a wallet and sign in. MetaMask will give you a 12-word secret recovery phrase – this is your wallet's private key, and must be kept safe and secret. If you lose this phrase, you will not be able to access your wallet. If someone else finds it, they will.

If you're already using MetaMask, we recommend creating a new account for testing with Replit. You can do this from the account menu, which appears when you click on the account avatar in the top right corner of the MetaMask interface.

Oracle design

An oracle is a hybrid system, made up of both contracts and traditional web server code. The contracts provide an interface for other contracts to request and receive data, and the web server code uses events and contract functions to respond to these requests and supply the required data. At a high level, the architecture looks like this:

Users interact with different smart contract protocols, such as decentralized exchanges or NFT markets. These protocols can source data from an oracle smart contract, which receives its data from off-chain data providers (these are usually some form of API).

In this tutorial, we will be building an oracle for random number generation, using the RANDOM.ORG API. If you've completed our ReplBot NFT tutorial, you'll know that true randomness is pretty much impossible to come by on the blockchain, and so an oracle is really the only solution for code that requires random numbers.

In much discussion and documentation of Ethereum oracles, the word "oracle" is used interchangeably to refer to three different things:

  1. Off-chain data providers
  2. Oracle contracts that bridge data onto the blockchain
  3. Complete solutions made up of 1 and 2

To avoid confusion, we'll use the following terms throughout this tutorial:

  1. Providers
  2. Oracle contracts
  3. Oracles

Caller contract

We'll start off by pretending that our oracle has already been built, and develop a contract that will request random numbers from it. This may sound like putting the cart before the horse, but developing this way will give us a clear idea of what we want from the finished product and how it will have to work.

This contract, which we'll name Caller, will be very bare-bones. All it's going to do is allow users to request random numbers and emit those numbers in events. At the end of this tutorial, you can expand Caller to do something more interesting with the numbers.

We'll design our oracle using Chainlink's Basic Request Model as a basis. As getting data from an oracle requires off-chain interaction, we won't be able to get our random number with a single function call. Instead, we'll implement a function to request a random number, which will be called by the contract's users, and a second function to fulfill a request, which will be called by the oracle contract. The request function will return a request ID that we can use to identify the final result. This is a similar pattern to callbacks in JavaScript.

Create a new directory in your repl called contracts. Then create a subdirectory at contracts/caller. Inside this subdirectory, create a new file named Caller.sol. Enter the following code into your new file:

// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT
pragma solidity ^0.8.2; import "@openzeppelin-solidity/contracts/access/Ownable.sol";
import "./IRandOracle.sol"; contract Caller is Ownable {
}

This Caller contract stub imports two dependencies:

  • OpenZeppelin's Ownable, an access control mix-in that allows us to implement functions that only the contract's owner (the address that deploys the contract) will be able to call.
  • A local contract called IRandOracle. This is an interface that tells this contract how to interact with the oracle contract.

Before we fill in Caller's logic, let's create that interface. Make a new file in the same directory named IRandOracle.sol, and add the following code to it:

// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT
pragma solidity ^0.8.2; interface IRandOracle { function requestRandomNumber() external returns (uint256);
}

That's it! Interfaces don't contain any implementation details, and don't even have to specify every external function in the contract they're referencing. As Caller will only call this one oracle contract function, that's the only one we have to specify.

Now let's go back to Caller.sol and implement some logic. Add the code below between your contract's opening and closing curly brace:

 IRandOracle private randOracle; mapping(uint256=>bool) requests; mapping(uint256=>uint256) results;

We first create a variable to reference our oracle contract, and then two mappings:

  • requests, which will keep track of active request IDs.
  • results, which will store the random numbers received for each request ID.

Then we can add some housekeeping functions:

 modifier onlyRandOracle() { require(msg.sender == address(randOracle), "Unauthorized."); _; } function setRandOracleAddress(address newAddress) external onlyOwner { randOracle = IRandOracle(newAddress); emit OracleAddressChanged(newAddress); }

First, we define the onlyRandOracle modifier, which we'll use to restrict access to our fulfillment function. It does this by using a require statement to throw an error if the function caller's address is not that of the oracle contract. Without that, any user would be able to submit "random" numbers of their chosing to fulfill our requests.

Second, we add an onlyOwner function (this is another modifier, defined in OpenZeppelin's Ownable) to set the address of the oracle contract we'll be using. As the contract owner, we'll be able to change the oracle address when necessary.

Our code creates an instance of our IRandOracle interface with the provided address, and then emits an event to let users know that a change has been made to the contract. Well-written contracts should emit events for configuration changes like this, so that their operations remain transparent to users.

With our housekeeping done, we can now write Caller's main functions below the definition of setRandOracleAddress(). First, getRandomNumber():

 function getRandomNumber() external { require(randOracle != IRandOracle(address(0)), "Oracle not initialized."); uint256 id = randOracle.requestRandomNumber(); requests[id] = true; emit RandomNumberRequested(id); }

Here we use a require statement to ensure that the contract's oracle is initialized. We do this by checking that it is not a contract at the null address, which is the address of uninitialized contract references. We then call requestRandomNumber(), the function that we declared in the IRandOracle interface. This function will return a request ID, which we mark as valid in our requests mapping. Finally, we emit an event to show that a random number has been requested.

Now we need to write the callback function. Add the following code below the function you added above:

 function fulfillRandomNumberRequest(uint256 randomNumber, uint256 id) external onlyRandOracle { require(requests[id], "Request is invalid or already fulfilled."); results[id] = randomNumber; delete requests[id]; emit RandomNumberReceived(randomNumber, id); }

When the oracle contract calls this function (which only it is allowed to do, per onlyRandOracle) it will supply the random number requested along with the request ID it's fufilling. The function will first check if the request ID is valid and then store the random number in the results mapping.

Now that the request has been fulfilled, it will also delete the request ID from requests, which is equivalent to setting it to false. This will ensure that only active requests are tracked.

Finally, our callback function emits an event to announce that the request has been fulfilled. In a more complex contract, this function would do more than just store the random number in a results mapping: for example, it might use the number to determine a lottery winner, or generate an attribute of an NFT.

Before we wrap up this contract, we need to define the events we've emitted above. Add the following code to the bottom of the contract body:

 event OracleAddressChanged(address oracleAddress); event RandomNumberRequested(uint256 id); event RandomNumberReceived(uint256 number, uint256 id);

Our caller contract is now complete. But it won't be able to do much of anything until we implement the oracle contract it depends on.

Oracle contract

If you take another look at the architecture diagram above, you'll notice that the oracle contract is intended to interact with multiple off-chain data providers. This is to ensure decentralization, a key attribute of robust smart contract protocols. If we relied on a single data provider for our random numbers, we'd be in trouble if that provider was compromised and the numbers it sent to us started being fixed, or if it had an outage and stopped returning anything.

So to minimize the impact of any single provider being compromised or going down, we'll implement functionality to source several different random numbers from several different providers, which we'll combine at the end using a bitwise XOR.

Create a new subdirectory in your repl at contracts/oracle. Inside this subdirectory, create a new file named RandOracle.sol. Enter the following code into your new file:

// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT
pragma solidity ^0.8.2; import "@openzeppelin/contracts/access/AccessControl.sol";
import "./ICaller.sol"; contract RandOracle is AccessControl {
}

This stub is quite similar to the code we started out with when writing Caller, having only two key differences:

  • Instead of Ownable, we import AccessControl from OpenZeppelin, which will allow us to implement role-based access control, a more complex and granular authorization model than we used in Caller. While we could have used this for Caller as well, it would have been overkill, and we'd like to keep our contracts as small as possible to save on deployment costs.
  • Instead of IRandOracle, we import the interface ICaller. You can probably guess what it will contain. Let's create it now, in a file named ICaller.sol within the same directory: 
// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT
pragma solidity ^0.8.2; interface ICaller { function fulfillRandomNumberRequest(uint256 randomNumber, uint256 id) external;
}

Like Caller, RandOracle only needs to know about a single function in the other contract.

Let's return to RandOracle.sol and define some state variables.

 bytes32 public constant PROVIDER_ROLE = keccak256("PROVIDER_ROLE");

First we define a name for our data provider role, in accordance with the AccessControl contract's documentation. We then define two variables which we'll use to manage multiple providers:

 uint private numProviders = 0; uint private providersThreshold = 1;

We use numProviders to store the total count of data providers we've added to the contract, and providersThreshold to define the minimum number of provider responses we need to consider a request fulfilled. For now, we've set providersThreshold to just one, opening ourselves up to the centralization risk mentioned above, but it will suffice for getting a basic demo up and running.

Next, we need to define some variables we'll use to deal with requests and responses. Enter the following code below the definitions you just added above:

 uint private randNonce = 0; mapping(uint256=>bool) private pendingRequests; struct Response { address providerAddress; address callerAddress; uint256 randomNumber; } mapping(uint256=>Response[]) private idToResponses;

Here we define:

  • randNonce, a cryptographic nonce we'll use to generate request IDs. This will be a simple counter that we increment every time requestRandomNumber() is called.
  • pendingRequests, a mapping of requests awaiting fulfillment, similar to requests in our Caller contract.
  • The Response struct, in which we'll store all the key details of each random number we receive from data providers: who requested the number, who provided the number, and the number itself.
  • idToResponses, a mapping of request IDs to arrays of Response structs. This will allow us to track responses per request.

Now let's define our contract's constructor, the function that will run when it's deployed.

 constructor() { _setupRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE, msg.sender); // make the deployer admin
 }

This function assigns AccessControl's DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE to the contract's deploying address, commonly called its owner. This role has the power to grant and revoke other roles.

Now we're ready to define requestRandomNumber(), the function we called from Caller. Add the following code below the constructor's definition:

 function requestRandomNumber() external returns (uint256) { require(numProviders > 0, " No data providers not yet added."); randNonce++; uint id = uint(keccak256(abi.encodePacked(block.timestamp, msg.sender, randNonce))) % 1000; pendingRequests[id] = true; emit RandomNumberRequested(msg.sender, id); return id; }

All this code does is generate a unique ID for the request, based on randNonce, adds that ID to pendingRequests, emits an event and returns the ID, similar to the concept of a support desk ticketing system. The require statement at the top of the code will revert if the contract's administrator has not yet added any data providers.

An off-chain data provider, which we'll create later on using Node.js, will watch the contract for RandomNumberRequested events and respond to them by submitting a random number to the contract function returnRandomNumber(), which will in turn return the number to the caller contract using fulfillRandomNumberRequest(). Let's start writing returnRandomNumber() now, just below requestRandomNumber():

 function returnRandomNumber(uint256 randomNumber, address callerAddress, uint256 id) external onlyRole(PROVIDER_ROLE) { require(pendingRequests[id], "Request not found."); // Add newest response to list
 Response memory res = Response(msg.sender, callerAddress, randomNumber); idToResponses[id].push(res); uint numResponses = idToResponses[id].length; }

Here, returnRandomNumber is a public function which we'll restrict to the addresses with the PROVIDER_ROLE. It takes three arguments: the random number, the address that made the initial request, and the request ID. In the code above, we ensure that the request ID is valid and then add the response to the array of responses for the request with this ID, stored in idToResponses. We then store the length of the array in numResponses, which we'll use to check if we've met the response threshold.

In the next part of the function, we'll combine all the responses we receive and submit the result back to the caller. Add the following code below the line that starts with uint numResponses:

 // Check if we've received enough responses
 if (numResponses == providersThreshold) { uint compositeRandomNumber = 0; // Loop through the array and combine responses
 for (uint i=0; i < idToResponses[id].length; i++) { compositeRandomNumber = compositeRandomNumber ^ idToResponses[id][i].randomNumber; // bitwise XOR
 } // Clean up
 delete pendingRequests[id]; delete idToResponses[id]; // Fulfill request
 ICaller(callerAddress).fulfillRandomNumberRequest(compositeRandomNumber, id); emit RandomNumberReturned(compositeRandomNumber, callerAddress, id); }

The code in this if block will only run when the final data provider submits its random number. It combines all the random numbers through a bitwise XOR, as taking an average would reduce the randomness. It then deletes the data for this request – without this step, data providers could keep submitting different random numbers and changing the result. It then fulfills the request by invoking the caller function's callback, and finally emits an event.

Functions in external contracts should almost always be invoked at or near the end of a given function, after all state change operations have been performed. This avoids the risk of reentrancy, a common smart contract bug that was the cause of the famous 2016 DAO hack.

Having now completed our oracle contract's main functions, we need to add a few housekeeping functions for the contract admin to manage data providers. We'll start with a function for adding new data providers. Add the following code at the bottom of the contract body:

 // Admin functions
 function addProvider(address provider) external onlyRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE) { require(!hasRole(PROVIDER_ROLE, provider), "Provider already added."); _grantRole(PROVIDER_ROLE, provider); numProviders++; emit ProviderAdded(provider); }

This code is quite straight-forward. Following a duplication check, it uses the _grantRole() function from AccessControl to assign PROVIDER_ROLE to the specified address, increments numProviders, and emits an event to let users know of a contract configuration change.

Note that we've used the internal function _grantRole() rather than the public function grantRole(): we can do this because internal functions are usable inside the same contract and all contracts which inherit from it. This is distinct from private functions, which cannot be called in child contracts.

Next, we add a function to remove data providers:

 function removeProvider(address provider) external onlyRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE) { require(!hasRole(PROVIDER_ROLE, provider), "Address is not a recognized provider."); require (numProviders > 1, "Cannot remove the only provider."); _revokeRole(PROVIDER_ROLE, provider); numProviders--; emit ProviderRemoved(provider); }

This function performs the reverse operation of the one above, with an extra check to ensure that the administrator does not remove all providers from the contract, rendering it unusable.

The last admin function we need is one that sets the provider threshold. Add the following code below the body of removeProvider():

 function setProvidersThreshold(uint threshold) external onlyRole(DEFAULT_ADMIN_ROLE) { require(threshold > 0, "Threshold cannot be zero."); providersThreshold = threshold; emit ProvidersThresholdChanged(providersThreshold); }

Finally, let's define the events we've emitted above. Add the following code to the bottom of the contract body:

 // Events
 event RandomNumberRequested(address callerAddress, uint id); event RandomNumberReturned(uint256 randomNumber, address callerAddress, uint id); event ProviderAdded(address providerAddress); event ProviderRemoved(address providerAddress); event ProvidersThresholdChanged(uint threshold);

Our oracle contract is complete. Next, we need to create a data provider to feed it random numbers.

Node.js data provider

We'll write our data provider as a simple Node.js application, using the Hardhat Ethereum development environment to help us interact with the blockchain. This environment provides a host of features for writing, testing, and deploying smart contracts, and for building applications that interface with the blockchain, such as this one. It includes a slightly modified version of the ethers library, which is the main component we'll be using.

As previously stated, our data provider will monitor the oracle contract for random number requests and submit responses to them. As JavaScript programs are single-threaded, we'll use a chunked queue structure to split the program's time between taking note of new requests and processing existing requests.

We'll need to install Hardhat before we can start developing. Open your repl's package.json file and add the following line to the bottom of the dependencies object (remember to add a comma to the end of the preceding line):

Create a new directory in your repl named provider. Inside that directory, create a JavaScript file named index.js. Add the following initial code to the file:

const hardhat = require('hardhat');
const axios = require('axios'); const MAX_RETRIES = 5;
const SLEEP_TIME = 2000;
const BATCH_SIZE = 3;

Here we import hardhat and axios, which we'll need to request random numbers from the RANDOM.ORG API.

The constants we've defined will be used as follows:

  • MAX_RETRIES is the maximum number of times we'll attempt to fulfill a request. Our ability to fulfill requests could be hampered by RANDOM.ORG experiencing an outage, or our data provider's wallet not having enough Ethereum to pay the gas cost of executing returnRandomNumber().
  • SLEEP_TIME is how long we'll pause between processing batches of the request queue to give our application a chance to add new requests to the queue.
  • BATCH_SIZE is how many requests we'll fufill between sleeps when processing the request queue.

Before we dive into our application's main code, let's create a function for getting a random number from RANDOM.ORG. Add the following code below your constant definitions:

async function requestRandomNumber() { const res = await axios({ url: 'https://www.random.org/integers/', params: { num: 1, min: 1, max: 1000, col: 1, base: 10, format: 'plain', rnd: 'new' }, method: 'get' }); return parseInt(res.data);
}

Here we use axios to construct and submit an HTTP request to RANDOM.ORG's integer generator, in accordance with their documentation. As axios is an asynchronous function, we must use the await keyword to receive its final return value. The same will be true of most functions we use from the ethers library.

Now let's start with the meat of our application. Add the following code to the bottom of the file:

async function main() { // Initialize account const [ dataProvider ] = await hardhat.ethers.getSigners(); // Initialize contract const oracleContractAddress = "ORACLE-CONTRACT-ADDRESS-HERE"; const oracleContractABI = require("./randOracleABI.json"); const oracleContract = new hardhat.ethers.Contract(oracleContractAddress, oracleContractABI, dataProvider);
} main();

The getSigners() function from Hardhat's modified ethers library retrieves Ethereum accounts based on a provided wallet. For frontend dapps, this will usually be a wallet extension like MetaMask, but for scripts like this one, we'll need to specify a private key. We'll do that later, when we combine everything together.

The next chunk of code initializes a Contract object. To connect to a contract and run its functions, we need two things: the contract's address, which shows us where to find it, and its Application Binary Interface (ABI), which tells us what functions it implements and what their parameters are. We'll leave these as placeholders for now.

Note that we've also specified dataProvider as an argument for our Contract object's constructor. This provides a default account to use when calling contract functions.

With that setup complete, we can start watching for requests and populating our queue. Add the following code to the bottom of your main() function:

 // Populate requests queue var requestsQueue = []; oracleContract.on("RandomNumberRequested", async (callerAddress, id) => { requestsQueue.push({ callerAddress, id }); });

Here we've initialized an empty requests queue and used oracleContract's on method to subscribe to the RandomNumberRequested event. Whenever a new event of this type is emitted by our contract, the code in our callback will execute, appending an object containing the event's arguments to requestsQueue.

That's the input part of our functionality done, so now we need the output. As mentioned above, we'll process the queue in chunks at regular intervals, so let's wrap this next bit of code in a [setInterval()] function. Add the following code to the bottom your main() function:

 // Poll and process requests queue at intervals setInterval(async() => { let processedRequests = 0; }, SLEEP_TIME);

We use processedRequests to track how many requests we've processed in this cycle, up to BATCH_SIZE. Let's start with a simple while loop, using the shift() method to pop the first element off the requests queue. Add the following code below the definition of processedRequests within the body of setInterval:

 while (requestsQueue.length > 0 && processedRequests < BATCH_SIZE) { const request = requestsQueue.shift(); }

Now we can process the request with the next bit of code, which you can add to the while loop, below the definition of request:

 let retries = 0; while (retries < MAX_RETRIES) { try { const randomNumber = await requestRandomNumber(); await oracleContract.returnRandomNumber(randomNumber, request.callerAddress, request.id); break; } catch (error) { retries++; } } processedRequests++;

We use a try... catch to smoothly handle any errors that occur. We want to be able to leave this program running without worrying about it crashing on an error.

First, this code attempts to request a random number and send it to our oracle contract by calling returnRandomNumber(). All contract function calls in ethers are asynchronous, so we must await them. If the operation is successful, we break out of the retry loop and move on to the next request. If not, we increment the retries counter and try again.

If we reach the maximum number of retries, we'll go to the next request without submitting anything to the oracle contract.

That's it for our data provider. The last thing we need to write before we can put it all together is a simple frontend for our Caller contract, providing a user-friendly way for people to request and recieve random numbers.

Caller contract frontend

Create a new folder in your repl called frontend and then create two files inside it: index.html and app.js. Add the following markup to frontend/index.html:

<!DOCTYPE html>
<html> <head> <title>Random Oracle Client</title> <style> .request { color: red; } .response { color: green; } </style> <head> <body> <button id="request-rand" class="button">Request random number</button><br> <pre id="request-id"></pre> <ul id="events"></ul> <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/web3@latest/dist/web3.min.js"></script> <script src="./callerABI.js"></script> <script src="./app.js"></script> </body>
</html>

In this file, we've created the following:

  • A button element for requesting new random numbers.
  • A pre element, in which we'll print the ID of the last request.
  • A ul element for logging contract events as they occur.

We've also linked our stylesheet at the top of the file and the following JavaScript files at the bottom:

  • web3.js, the library we'll be using to interact with the blockchain.
  • The Caller contract's ABI, which we'll get once we compile and deploy it.
  • Our app.js file, which will contain the frontend's logic.

Open frontend/app.js and enter the code skeleton:

App = { callerAddress: "FILL-ME-IN", callerContract: null, init: async function() {},
} App.init();

This App object will contain all of the status and functionality we'll implement. Note that this frontend will only interact with Caller, and not RandOracle. We'll come back and enter the contract address in the next section, once we've deployed everything.

The init() function will connect to the blockchain and initialize our application's behavior. Populate your empty function body as follows:

 init: async function() { if (window.ethereum) { await window.ethereum.request({ method: 'eth_requestAccounts' }); window.web3 = new Web3(window.ethereum); App.callerContract = new web3.eth.Contract(callerABI, callerAddress); // Switch networks App.switchToReplitTestnet(); } },

This code will interface with MetaMask and show a pop-up window asking the user to connect their wallet to our dapp. If the user accepts, we'll instantiate a Web3 object using their account details. As with ethers, the majority of Web3's functions are asynchronous.

Because we're building on the Replit Testnet, we need to prompt the user to switch to this network, which we'll do in the function switchToReplitTestnet(). Add the definition for this function below the definition of init:

 switchToReplitTestnet: function() { window.ethereum.request({ method: "wallet_addEthereumChain", params: [ { chainId: "0x7265706c", chainName: "Replit Testnet", rpcUrls: ["https://eth.replit.com"], iconUrls: [ "https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/b/b2/Repl.it_logo.svg", ], nativeCurrency: { name: "Replit ETH", symbol: "RΞ", decimals: 18, }, }, ], }); },

This code provides the details MetaMask needs to prompt the user to switch networks.

Set up done, we can move on to app functionality. We'll start with a function that calls the Caller contract's getRandomNumber() function.

 // contract interactions getRandomNumber: async function() { const accounts = await web3.eth.getAccounts(); const account = accounts[0]; // Request random number & return request ID return (await App.callerContract.methods.getRandomNumber().send({from: account})); },

Here we use web3.eth.getAccounts() to find the user's wallet address, which we use to call the contract function getRandomNumber(). Note that the pattern and syntax for calling functions in Web3 differs substantially from the syntax we used in ethers.

Next we need a function that subscribes to contract events and prepends their details to our webpage's event log. Add the following code:

 subscribeToContractEvents: function() { App.callerContract.events.RandomNumberRequested(async (err, event) => { if (err) console.error('Error on event', err) // Create list item let reqEventLi = document.createElement("li"); reqEventLi.classList.add("request"); reqEventLi.innerHTML = `Random number requested, ID: ${event.returnValues.id}`; // Add to top of list const eventLog = document.getElementById("events"); eventLog.prepend(reqEventLi); }); App.callerContract.events.RandomNumberReceived(async (err, event) => { if (err) console.error('Error on event', err) // Create list item let recEventLi = document.createElement("li"); recEventLi.classList.add("response"); recEventLi.innerHTML = `Random number received for ID ${event.returnValues.id}: ${event.returnValues.number}`; // Add to top of list const eventLog = document.getElementById("events"); eventLog.prepend(recEventLi); }); },

This code is similar to the event subscription code we used in our data provider, but uses the web3.js Contract.events property. It waits for new contract events to be emitted and adds a record of each one to the top of the page's events list.

Next we need to work with JavaScript events and bind our random number button to the contract interaction function we wrote above. Add the following code:

 // interface bindBrowserEvents: function () { const requestButton = document.getElementById("request-rand"); requestButton.addEventListener("click", async function() { const transaction = await App.getRandomNumber(); const requestID = document.getElementById("request-id"); requestID.innerHTML = `Submitted! Request ID: ${transaction.events.RandomNumberRequested.returnValues.id}`; }); },

This code will ensure that when we click the button, the contract function will be invoked, and the request ID in its emitted event will be printed to the page.

Before we wrap this file up, we need to invoke both of these event-related functions in our init() function. Amend the function as follows:

 init: async function() { if (window.ethereum) { await window.ethereum.request({ method: 'eth_requestAccounts' }); window.web3 = new Web3(window.ethereum); App.callerContract = new web3.eth.Contract(callerABI, App.callerAddress); // Switch networks App.switchToReplitTestnet(); } // NEW CODE BELOW App.subscribeToContractEvents(); App.bindBrowserEvents(); },

We've now got everything we need to get both oracle and oracle client up and running. In the next section, we'll deploy our contracts and connect up everything we've built.

Putting it all together

We've created a lot of different pieces in the sections above. It's now time to put the puzzle together.

Compiling and deploying the contracts

We'll start by compiling and deploying our contracts. But first, we need to make a change to our dependencies. Our oracle contract relies on code from version 4.6.0 of OpenZeppelin, so we'll need to edit our repl's package.json to ensure that we import that version. Open package.json now, find the dependency @openzeppelin/contracts and change the version number next to it to ^4.6.0.

To compile our contracts in the Solidity starter repository, we'll need to import them into contract.sol by their full paths. Delete the current contents of contract.sol and replace them with the following:

// SPDX-License-Identifier: MIT
pragma solidity ^0.8.2; import "/home/runner/YOUR-REPL-NAME-HERE/contracts/caller/Caller.sol";
import "/home/runner/YOUR-REPL-NAME-HERE/contracts/oracle/RandOracle.sol";

Enter your repl's name as indicated. Then run your repl and wait for the Solidity starter web interface to appear, and for both contracts to compile.

Once we've compiled, we can deploy, but for that we'll need some funds. Connect your MetaMask wallet to the web interface and switch to the Replit Testnet. Then click the link to get 1 ETH for testing. Wait until 1 ETH shows up in your wallet balance on the top right of the page.


Now you can deploy your contracts. Select "Caller" from the drop-down box and click Deploy. Approve the MetaMask pop-up that appears. Then do the same with "RandOracle".

Once both contracts have been deployed, they will show up as expandable boxes below the drop-down box. Expand them and take a look at the functions available in each.

In practice, the Caller and RandOracle contracts would usually be deployed by different addresses, potentially belonging to entirely different teams, but we're using a single one to avoid having to log in and out of MetaMask over and over.

Initializing the contracts

Next, we need to connect Caller to RandOracle. Find the setRandOracleAddress function in Caller. Then click on the address at the bottom of RandOracle's dropdown box to copy it and paste it in as the value for newAddress. Then click Run.

We're done setting up our contracts, but leave your repl running with the Solidity starter web interface open, as we'll need it to set up our data provider application and client frontend.

Preparing the data provider

Before we do anything to integrate our data provider with the blockchain, let's include axios in our repl's package.json file. Add the following line to the bottom of the dependencies object if axios is not already included as a dependency (remember to add a comma to the end of the preceding line):

With that done, we need to copy RandOracle's ABI into a file where the data provider can see it. Click on Copy ABI next to RandOracle's address to load the ABI into your clipboard. Then create a new file in provider named randOracleABI.json and paste the contents of your clipboard into it.

Now open index.js, find the line beginning with const oracleContractAddress, and replace the placeholder string with the address of the deployed RandOracle contract.

The last thing we need to do to get our data provider working is to provide it with a wallet. The wallet we use for MetaMask is locked by a password, so it can't easily be used for automated operations. In any case, it's more hygenic for us to create a dedicated wallet for our data provider. We'll do this with a simple Node.js script and the ethereumjs-wallet library.

Open your repl's package.json file. Add the following line to the bottom of the dependencies object (remember to add a comma to the end of the preceding line):

 "ethereumjs-wallet": "^1.0.2"

Now create a new file named walletGen.js. Populate it with the code below:

const wallet = require('ethereumjs-wallet').default; const addressData = wallet.generate(); console.log(`Private key: ${addressData.getPrivateKeyString()}`);
console.log(`Address: ${addressData.getAddressString()}`);

Stop and run your repl. Navigate to the Shell tab in bottom-right panel and run the command node walletGen.js. You should see two long strings of letters and numbers appear beneath your command. The first is your new wallet's private key, and the second is your new wallet's address.

Anyone who knows this private key can control the wallet, so we'll need to keep it safe. Open the Secrets tab on your repl's sidebar. Create new key named DATAPROVIDER_PRIVATE_KEY and paste in the private key as its value. Click the button "Add new value" to save the entry.

Now we need to add the address of the new wallet as a data provider for our RandOracle contract. In your repl's web browser, navigate to the addProvider function in RandOracle. Paste in the address of your new wallet as the value for provider. Then click Run.

Our new wallet will also need some ETH to send random number responses to the blockchain. We can send it some of ours using MetaMask. Open the MetaMask extension interface and click on the button marked Send. It should be right underneath your balance. Paste the data provider's wallet address into the search bar that appears. Then enter a small number into the Amount field, such as 0.2, click Next, and confirm the transaction.

Now we need to configure Hardhat, so that it knows what network to connect to (the Replit Testnet) and what wallet to use (the one we just created) when running our data provider application. First add the following line to the bottom of the dependencies object (remember to add a comma to the end of the preceding line):

"@nomiclabs/hardhat-waffle": "^2.0.3"

Create a file named hardhat.config.js with the following contents:

require("@nomiclabs/hardhat-waffle"); // You need to export an object to set up your config
// Go to https://hardhat.org/config/ to learn more /** * @type import('hardhat/config').HardhatUserConfig */
module.exports = { networks: { replit: { url: `https://eth.replit.com`, accounts: [process.env.DATAPROVIDER_PRIVATE_KEY] } }, solidity: "0.8.2"
}

That's it for our data provider. Now we just need to prepare the client frontend.

Preparing the client frontend

We need to fill in the ABI and address for Caller. Click on Copy ABI next to Caller's address to load the ABI into your clipboard. Then create a new file in frontend named callerABI.js and add the following code to it:

Paste the contents of your clipboard after the equals sign. We've already included this file in frontend/index.html, so now we just need the contract address. Open frontend/app.js and paste the address of Caller in place of the placeholder value of callerAddress near the top of the file.

We'll also need to create a simple Node.js Express application to render the frontend. Create a file named frontend.js in your repl and add the following code to it:

const express = require('express');
const app = express();
const PORT = 433; app.use(express.static('frontend')); app.listen(PORT, () => console.log(`Server listening on port: ${PORT}`));

Running the repl

Now that we have everything connected, we want to replace the Solidity starter interface with our data provider application and client frontend. To do this, make sure that hidden files are showing and open .replit.

Replace run = "node tools" with the following:

This will make our repl run a Bash script instead of single command, which is useful when we need to run more than one thing. Create a file named start.sh and add the following code to it:

# Start data provider in the background
npx hardhat run provider --network replit & # Start client frontend
node frontend.js

We must run the provider as a Hardhat script rather than a plain Node.js application to use our Hardhat configuration. The & symbol will make the data provider run in the background, so that we can also start the frontend.

Our client and oracle are finally both ready to roll. Stop your repl and rerun it. You should see our frontend in the repl browser. Click the button to request a random number and watch the events come in.

Where next?

In this tutorial, we've built a simplified version of Chainlink's Verifiable Random Function oracle. While ours doesn't provide the same level of robust security, building it has given us insight into how smart contracts can work with off-chain data, and we can use it for other projects in the future, or let other Solidity developers on Replit use it for their projects.

Some next steps you might want to try:

  • Create a few new repls containing just the data provider code and give each one its own wallet. Add the addresses as data providers to RandOracle, increase the providers threshold, and see how the contract works with more than one provider.
  • Build something more sophisticated on top of Client. For example, you could adapt the ReplBot NFT contracts to use this oracle as a source of randomness. Or you could write something like a lottery contract.
  • Experiment with different data sources. An oracle can be a source for any data you can think of, from stock prices to the weather.

You can find our repl here.
